description:
## Goal

Close the four gaps surfaced by reviewing the fz-cty epic in flight:

1. **The procbin/sharedbin abstraction is leaky** — `shared_bin_live_count` is on the public API for tests; `live_count_lock` serializes test code; `Heap::alloc_procbin` is `unsafe` with an implicit refcount-ownership contract; bitstring dispatch helpers reach into ProcBin layout from `fz_value.rs`. Six modules share knowledge they shouldn't.

2. **The constant-binary path is half-done** — `Prim::ConstBitstring` puts bytes in `.rodata` but still memcpys them into a heap-allocated SharedBin at runtime, and the fold pass narrowly accepts only 8-bit unsigned literal bytes.

3. **Refcount ordering is unverified** — canonical-Arc pattern by claim, no loom or stress test that would catch a real ordering bug.

4. **The 6 MiB heap-object cap is a punt, not a lift** — objects above `SIZE_TABLE[last]` panic with "exceeds largest size class" instead of being routed through a real large-object mechanism.

## Unifying idea

A `SharedBin` carries its own destructor function pointer. When refcount hits zero, the runtime calls the bin's destructor — no hardcoded freeing logic, no sentinel values, no two-step retain/release. Heap-allocated bins get `shared_bin_destructor_heap` (frees the byte box + SharedBin box). Compiler-baked static bins get `shared_bin_destructor_noop`. Future kinds (mmap'd, sub-binary, FFI-wrapped) slot in without touching the dispatch.

That single change — destructor-as-data — makes the abstraction truly polymorphic. The same retain/release primitives work for every flavour of SharedBin; the same MSO sweep handles them all; the same loom proof covers all paths. Most of the warts on the list are symptoms of not having this property.

Combined with intrusive MSO list links (using the 8 bytes ProcBin currently wastes on padding) and the layout reshape from `Box<[u8]>` to `(bytes_ptr, bytes_len, destructor)`, the procbin module becomes a sealed, typed, allocation-safe abstraction. Outside the module, no code reads ProcBin offsets, no code calls raw retain/release, no code reaches into refcounts.

## Subticket DAG

Strict, atomic, testable per CLAUDE.md:

```
.1 (consolidation) ─┬─► .2 (static binaries + widened fold)
                    │
                    └─► .3 (loom verification)

.4 (heap fragments) — independent
```

- **.1 — procbin consolidation.** Module ownership of the abstraction. New SharedBin layout with destructor pointer. Intrusive MSO list. Typed `SharedBinHandle` (Arc-shaped) and `ProcBin` newtype. Helpers move home. `live_count_lock` deleted, `shared_bin_live_count` becomes `pub(crate)`. All call sites updated to safe APIs.
- **.2 — static .rodata SharedBins + widened const-fold.** Codegen emits a 40-byte static SharedBin in `.data` (with refcount=1 anchor, two relocations to bytes payload and noop destructor). Widened fold drives `BitWriter` at compile time, accepting any (size, endian, sign) for Integer and Float fields.
- **.3 — loom verification of retain/release ordering.** Sync abstraction module routes atomics through std or loom under `cfg(loom)`. Test exercises two-thread retain/release; loom verifies no UAF, no double-free, destructor called exactly once.
- **.4 — heap fragments for genuine large-object support.** Per-Heap `fragments: Vec<Fragment>` with mark-bit liveness during Cheney. Oversized allocations route through fragments instead of panicking. Cap lift is real.

## Invariants

- Outside `runtime/src/procbin.rs` (or `shared_bin.rs` if renamed in .1), no code references SharedBin layout, ProcBin offsets, refcounts, or live-count instrumentation.
- Every retain on a SharedBin matches exactly one release, except for the perpetual anchor reference on static SharedBins (which the program binary owns; never released).
- The destructor is a permanent property of each SharedBin — set at allocation, never changed.
- The MSO list is exactly the set of live ProcBins on a given heap; the post-Cheney sweep maintains this exactly.
- Fragments don't move during Cheney; their liveness is the visited-mark; survivors persist across GC, dead fragments are freed.

## Why this finishes the story

After fz-cty, the off-heap binary system worked but the abstraction wasn't sealed and the cap wasn't really lifted. After this epic, both are true. The polymorphic destructor pattern unifies "heap-allocated SharedBin" and "compiler-baked static SharedBin" as instances of the same abstraction, eliminating special cases in retain/release. The widened fold catches every statically-knowable bitstring (not just byte-literal sequences). The loom test gives a real correctness witness for the atomic ordering claims. And heap fragments let the runtime accept any single-object size up to the system's malloc capacity — the cap stops being something to "punt past 6 MiB" and starts being a true natural limit.
title: [EPIC] shared-heap polish: sealed procbin, .rodata binaries via destructor pointer, true large-object support, loom verification
